originalTitle: These women fought in Ethiopia's last civil war and warn against another one
title: Brave Women Ask for Peace in Ethiopia!
content:
Hello, awesome kids! It’s your kid-friendly reporter here, ready to share a very important story from our amazing continent, Africa! Today, we're going to talk about a beautiful country called Ethiopia. It's a place with sunny skies, tall mountains, and super friendly people. But guess what? A little while ago, something very sad happened there. There was a big, big fight, like a very long argument between grown-ups, but much more serious. We call these kinds of big fights a "war" or "conflict." It made many people very sad and changed their lives. Today, we're going to hear from some super brave women who were part of that big fight. They have a very important message for everyone, especially for the people in Ethiopia. They want to make sure everyone chooses peace!

Let's Explore Ethiopia!

First, let's learn a little bit about Ethiopia! It's a country right here in Africa, on the eastern side. It's famous for many cool things! Did you know that Ethiopia is where coffee comes from? Yes, the grown-ups' favorite morning drink started there! It also has ancient stories, amazing churches carved into rocks, and super fast runners! It's a land of many different people, all living together. Imagine a country with lots of different languages, yummy foods, and colorful clothes! That's Ethiopia! **Did you know?** Ethiopia is one of the oldest countries in the world! People have lived there for a very, very long time.

A Time of Sadness and a Big Fight

Sometimes, even in beautiful places, people can have big disagreements. And sometimes, these disagreements can turn into a really big, sad fight. This happened in a part of Ethiopia called Tigray. It was a very difficult time. When there's a big fight like this, many things stop working. Schools might close, and it can be hard to get food or medicines. Houses can get broken, and families have to leave their homes to find a safe place. It makes everyone feel worried and scared. No one likes to feel scared, right?

Brave Women Who Helped

During this big fight, there were many brave people who tried to help their families and their country. And guess what? Many of them were women! Yes, strong, courageous women! They were called "fighters" because they joined the effort to protect their homes and their communities. They were like superheroes, but in real life, facing very tough times. They had to be very strong, both inside and out. It was not easy at all. These women saw many difficult things. They saw how the fight made everyone's lives harder. They saw friends and family get hurt. They saw their towns and villages change. Even after the big fight stopped, the memories stayed with them. It’s like when you have a bad dream, and even after you wake up, you still remember how scary it felt. These women feel that way about the big fight. **Did you know?** In many parts of Africa, women are very strong leaders in their communities. They help make sure families are safe and children go to school!

Their Important Message: No More Fighting!

Now, these brave women have a very, very important message. They want everyone in Ethiopia, and all over the world, to hear it. Their message is simple: "No more fighting! We want peace!" They remember how hard and sad the big fight was. They don't want anyone else to go through that again. They are worried that the fighting might start again, and they are saying, "Please, no! Let's choose peace instead." They remember how their lives were "destroyed," which just means they were broken and made very, very hard by the fight. Homes were broken, schools were closed, and it was hard to find food. They want to protect everyone from having their lives broken like that again. They are speaking up because they care so much about their country and its people. They want everyone to be safe and happy.

What is Peace and Why is it Good?

So, what is "peace"? Peace is a super lovely feeling! It means no more fighting, no more yelling, and no more being scared. It means everyone can be friends and live together happily. When there is peace, children can go to school every day. Farmers can grow their food safely. Doctors and nurses can help sick people without worrying. Families can stay together in their homes. It means smiles, laughter, and helping each other. These brave women want all of this for Ethiopia. They want children to play and learn without fear. They want families to be safe and happy. They know that peace is the best way for a country to grow strong and for all its people to be healthy and happy. **Did you know?** The word "peace" is important in many languages. In Ethiopia, people speak Amharic, and the word for peace is "Selam!"

Why This Story Matters to All of Us

Even though Ethiopia might seem far away, this story is important for all of us. When people live in peace, everyone benefits. We can learn from these brave women. They are teaching us that even after sad times, we can hope for a better future and work for peace. They are reminding us that talking and sharing can stop big fights from happening. We can all wish for peace for Ethiopia and for every place in the world. We can try to be kind to our friends and family, and help everyone get along. This is how we can make our own little world, and the big wide world, a more peaceful place! So, let's remember the brave women of Ethiopia. They are heroes who want to make sure that no child, no family, and no country has to go through a big, sad fight again. Let's all hope and work for a world where peace always wins! Thank you for listening, wonderful kids!
summary: Brave women who experienced a big fight in Ethiopia are asking everyone to choose peace instead, so no one has to be sad or scared again.
whyMatters: This story matters because it teaches us that fighting makes people sad, and choosing peace helps everyone live happily, go to school, and grow strong together.

originalTitle: Conservative Anglicans pull back from electing rival to Archbishop of Canterbury
title: Big Church News! Friends Decide to Talk Instead of Choose a New Leader!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends across Africa! It's your kid-friendly reporter here, ready to share some super interesting news from the big wide world. Guess what? Sometimes, even grown-ups in charge of churches have different ideas, just like you and your friends might have different ideas about playing a game! But the really good news is when people decide to talk and be kind, even when they don't agree. And that's exactly what happened in a special church group called the Anglicans.

What's This Big News About?

Imagine you have a big family, and some members of the family had a big plan to choose a brand-new leader because they didn't quite agree with the old one. But then, they decided to *stop* that plan! Yes, they pulled back, which means they changed their minds and decided not to choose their own leader after all. This is a very important step because it means they want to keep talking and finding ways to be together, even with different ideas.

Who Are the Anglicans?

The Anglicans are a very big group of churches all around the world, including many, many churches right here in Africa! They are like a huge family of churches that share similar ways of worshiping and believing. Think of it like different schools in a big city – they all teach children, but they might have slightly different uniforms or school songs. Anglican churches are like that, all part of one big family. Did you know? There are Anglican churches in over 165 countries! That's a lot of church families!

Meet the Super-Duper Leader!

In this big Anglican family, there is a very important person called the Archbishop of Canterbury. Wow, that's a long name, right? You can think of him like the head teacher for all the Anglican churches in many parts of the world. He helps everyone work together and stay connected. His name is Archbishop Justin Welby, and he's a very kind leader who wants everyone to get along. Did you know? The Archbishop of Canterbury lives in a very old building in England! He travels all over the world to visit different Anglican churches, even here in Africa.

What Was the Big Idea Some People Had?

So, some groups of Anglicans, who like things to stay very traditional and old-fashioned in the church, had an idea. They were thinking about choosing their *own* very important leader. It would be like choosing a second head teacher for some of the schools in the big city, just for their group of schools. This was because they had some really different ideas about how the church should be run.

Why Did They Want a New Leader?

The main reason for this big idea was about women. Some people in the Anglican church family believe that women should not be priests or very important leaders called bishops. A bishop is like a super-duper pastor who looks after many churches in an area. Other parts of the Anglican church family, including the Archbishop of Canterbury, think that women *can* be wonderful priests and bishops, just like men. Did you know? In many parts of Africa, women are amazing leaders in their communities, in schools, and in businesses! They help make things better for everyone.

What is a Priest and a Bishop?

Let's make it simple! A priest is someone who helps lead church services, teaches about God, and helps people in their lives. You might call them a pastor or a reverend. A bishop is like a very special leader who helps many priests and many churches in a bigger area. They help make important decisions and make sure everyone is doing well. So, whether women can be priests or bishops is a very important question for many people in the church.

What Happened Next? They Changed Their Minds!

Now for the happy news! The groups that were thinking about choosing their own new leader decided *not* to do it. They said, “Hold on! Maybe we should not rush into choosing a new leader right now.” This is a really big deal because it shows they want to try harder to talk to everyone else in the Anglican family, even if they have different ideas. They decided that it's better to keep talking and praying together than to separate and have different leaders. Did you know? Changing your mind can sometimes be a really strong and good thing to do, especially if it helps everyone get along better!

Why is Talking So Important?

Imagine you and your friend both want to play with the same toy. If you both just grab it, you might argue. But if you talk about it and decide to share, or take turns, then everyone is happier! It's the same for big groups of people. Talking helps everyone understand each other's feelings and ideas. It helps them find ways to work together, even when they don't agree on everything. Did you know? In many African cultures, talking and listening to elders and each other is a very important way to solve problems and keep peace in the community!

What Happens Now?

Now, because these groups decided not to choose a new leader, it means they are going to spend more time talking with the Archbishop of Canterbury and other leaders in the Anglican church family. They want to see if they can find ways to understand each other better and work together, even with their different ideas about women leaders. This news is important because it shows that even when people have big disagreements, choosing to talk and listen can be the best path forward. It's about being patient, kind, and trying to keep the big church family together. And that, my friends, is a wonderful lesson for all of us, no matter where we are in Africa or the world! So let's all try to talk and listen to each other today, just like these church leaders are trying to do!
summary: Some church groups who disagreed with other church leaders, especially about women being leaders, decided not to choose their own new leader and will instead keep talking and working together.
whyMatters: This is important because it means different church groups are trying to talk and be friends, even when they have different ideas, instead of separating. It shows that talking and listening is a good way to solve problems and stay together.

content: The M23, which controls the coltan-rich area, has not commented and the death toll is hard to verify.

originalTitle: 'We have been preparing': Why the boots on the ground in Iran could be Kurdish
title: Friends Far Away: News from Our World!
content:
Hello, wonderful friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, sending sunshine from Africa! Today, we're going on an adventure to learn about some special people far, far away. We're going to talk about friends who live in a part of the world called the Middle East. It's a place with beautiful mountains and lots of history. These friends want to make their homes even better, just like we all want our classrooms and playgrounds to be the best!

Who Are Our Kurdish Friends?

Imagine a group of people who share the same language, songs, and yummy food. These are our Kurdish friends! They are a very old and special group of people. They live in a place that is like a big, beautiful land that touches different countries. Some of them live in a country called Iran. Others live in a country nearby called Iraq. They love their land very much. Did you know? Many people around the world live in places where their land touches more than one country! This means they might have family living just a little bit away in a different country. It's like having your cousins live in the next town, but imagine if that next town was in a whole new country!

Why Are They Living in a Neighbor's House?

Sometimes, people need to leave their homes for a little while. This happens when they want things to change and be fairer in their own country. Our Kurdish friends from Iran are living in a country next door, called Iraq. They are there because they want to help make things better in their first home, Iran. We call this 'living in exile.' It means they are living away from their home country, but they hope to go back one day. They want peace and happiness for everyone.

What Are 'Opposition Groups'?

Think about when you and your friends have great ideas to make your school or your playground better. Maybe you want more swings, or a new game to play! You talk about your ideas with your teacher or your parents. These Kurdish friends are like that, but for their whole country. They are called 'opposition groups' because they have different ideas about how their country, Iran, should be run. They want to speak up for their people. They hope their ideas will bring good changes and make life safer and happier for everyone.

Talking to the News!

How do we learn about these friends and their ideas? Through news reporters! There's a big news company called the BBC. They are like very good storytellers who travel all around the world. The BBC reporters went to talk to our Kurdish friends. They asked them questions about their plans and their hopes. News companies help us understand what is happening in different places and with different people. It's how we learn about the world. Did you know? News reporters work very hard to find out facts and share stories with everyone. They help us stay connected to people all over our big planet Earth!

Plans to Visit Home: Crossing the Border

Imagine a line on the ground. On one side is your house, and on the other side is your friend's house. Now imagine a much bigger line. This line is called a 'border.' It shows where one country stops and another country begins. Our Kurdish friends in Iraq told the BBC that they have 'plans to cross the border.' This means they want to go across that special line. They want to go from Iraq back into Iran. Why do they want to do this? They want to go back to their homes and help their people directly. They want to share their ideas for making things better inside Iran. It's a big step for them, and they are thinking very carefully about it. They are getting ready for their journey.

'Not Yet!' They Said

The news reporters asked them a very important question. They asked, 'Have you already crossed the border into Iran?' Our Kurdish friends said, 'No, not yet!' They told the BBC that they have plans, but they haven't started their journey back into Iran yet. This means they are still preparing. It's like when you pack your bag for a school trip. You have all your things ready, but the bus hasn't come to pick you up yet. They are still getting everything organised for their big mission to help their people. They want everyone to know that they are thinking and planning carefully. They want to do things in a way that is good and safe for everyone involved. Their message is clear: they are preparing, but they are not there yet.

Why Is This Story Important for Us?

This story from far away is important for all of us here in Africa and everywhere else! It teaches us that there are many different people in the world. Everyone wants to live safely and happily in their homes. Sometimes, people need to work hard and speak up to make their homes better. Learning about our Kurdish friends helps us understand different cultures and ideas. It helps us remember that we are all part of one big global family. When we learn about others, we learn to be kind and understand people who are different from us. Did you know? Understanding people from different countries helps us make our whole world a friendlier place! It's like making new friends in your neighborhood, but on a much bigger scale! We send our good wishes to our Kurdish friends as they work to make their homes happy and peaceful.
summary: Kurdish friends living in another country say they are planning to go back home to Iran to help, but they haven't crossed the border yet.
whyMatters: This story shows us that people sometimes need to leave their homes to make things better, and it helps us learn about different people and places in the world, reminding us to be kind and understand everyone.

originalTitle: US asked Ukraine for help fighting Iranian drones, Zelensky says
title: US asked Ukraine for help fighting Iranian drones, Zelensky says
content: Ukraine's president says Kyiv will only help if doing so does not deplete its own air defences.
